posts.title: Event Complex: an LLM-run market for 50 companies
posts.author: Shaurya Garg
posts.intro: What happens when 50 companies make strategic decisions in real time and an LLM runs the market? I built the engine that found out.
posts.quote: The hardest part wasn't the AI — it was keeping 100+ people's decisions consistent while the LLMs churned in the background.

sections.heading: The idea
sections.body:
Event Complex is an LLM-powered consulting and market simulation platform. Teams representing 50 simulated companies make strategic business decisions that move company valuation, stock prices, and assets — all in real time.

Instead of a static case study, every choice a team makes feeds back into a living market that the other teams then have to react to.

sections.heading: The hard part: scale
sections.body:
The backend had to support 100+ concurrent users while orchestrating load-balanced LLM API calls and synchronizing real-time state across every team.

That meant request orchestration so no single LLM call blocked the room, and a state layer that stayed consistent even as dozens of decisions landed at once.

sections.heading: What I built
sections.body:
A decision-evaluation pipeline runs each team's strategy through LLM-driven workflows to dynamically simulate the market and business outcomes.

Stack: Python, FastAPI, PostgreSQL, LLM APIs, WebSockets.

posts.title: Perplexed: four C-suite chatbots for 200+ people
posts.author: Shaurya Garg
posts.intro: An AI consulting and audit simulation that put 200+ participants in a room with a company's entire executive team — except the executives were LLMs.

sections.heading: The setup
sections.body:
Built for Saturnalia, Perplexed hosted 200+ participants diagnosing real problem statements and building case solutions through realistic executive interactions.

Each participant could interrogate the leadership, and the leadership answered like the people who actually run the company.

sections.heading: Grounding the personas
sections.body:
I engineered an LLM backend of 4 distinct chatbots, each simulating a different C-suite persona. To keep them from hallucinating, every persona was grounded on vectorized company documents via Pinecone and LangChain.

So the CFO answered from the financials, the CTO from the tech docs — retrieval-augmented, not made up.

sections.heading: The frontend
sections.body:
I built the full Next.js / TanStack frontend and wired Google Gemini API calls for the simulated executive responses.

Stack: Next.js, TanStack, LangChain, Pinecone, Google Gemini.

posts.title: CitySaver: teaching buildings to share power
posts.author: Shaurya Garg
posts.intro: A real-time microgrid simulation where multiple buildings learn to coordinate energy decisions — and cut carbon by doing it.

sections.heading: The problem
sections.body:
Buildings consume power, generate solar, and emit carbon independently. Left alone, they don't coordinate — and the grid pays for it.

I wanted to model what happens when they cooperate.

sections.heading: Multi-agent reinforcement learning
sections.body:
Using the CityLearn environment, I built a platform that models energy consumption, solar generation, and carbon emissions across multiple buildings.

Then I integrated a Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning system (GridHarmony) so buildings coordinate energy decisions for grid-level optimization — reaching a 2.51% improvement in carbon emissions.

sections.heading: Under the hood
sections.body:
A simulation engine computes building-level consumption, renewable utilization, and carbon emissions from real dataset timesteps.

Stack: Python, FastAPI, CityLearn, MARL, WebSockets.

posts.title: KAIRA: building a robot that knows who it's talking to
posts.author: Shaurya Garg
posts.intro: Say "Hey KAIRA" and a robot wakes up, recognises your face, and answers from what it actually knows. Here's how it comes together.

sections.heading: Wake, see, respond
sections.body:
KAIRA is a Satbot project: a robot with a RAG architecture that recognises people and communicates, activated by the wake phrase "Hey KAIRA".

Computer vision handles the recognition; retrieval-augmented generation handles the answers, so it responds from a real knowledge base instead of guessing.

sections.heading: Reliable communication
sections.body:
The pieces talk over a Subscriber-Publisher architecture with socket-based communication for reliable, real-time interaction — so vision, speech, and the language model stay in sync without stepping on each other.

I also contributed to the design and development of the robot's user interface.

Stack: RAG, Pub-Sub architecture, sockets, computer vision.
